skill.name: network-101
skill.description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "set up a web server", "configure HTTP or HTTPS", "perform SNMP enumeration", "configure SMB shares", "test network services", or ne...
skill.summary: Configure network services for penetration testing lab environments. Set up HTTP, HTTPS, SNMP, and SMB services for hands-on security testing practice.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a legitimate penetration testing education skill for setting up lab environments. Static findings mostly represent false positives (legitimate admin commands, test URLs). However, the skill configures vulnerable services (default SNMP communities, chmod 777) that pose real risks if used improperly. Requires warnings about authorized use only in isolated lab environments.

medium_findings.title: Overly Permissive File Permissions
medium_findings.description: Skill instructs using chmod 777 on Samba share directories (lines 176, 329). This creates world-writable directories that could be exploited if applied to production systems.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: chmod 777 is explicitly shown in the skill code, but context is for anonymous SMB shares in lab environment

medium_findings.title: Default SNMP Community Strings
medium_findings.description: Skill configures SNMP with default community strings 'public' and 'private' (lines 127-128). These are well-known credentials that could expose systems if deployed improperly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Default SNMP communities are explicitly configured in the skill for lab testing

medium_findings.title: Potential Misuse for Unauthorized Access
medium_findings.description: Skill teaches enumeration techniques (nmap, snmpwalk, enum4linux) that could be misused against systems without authorization. While framed as lab setup, the techniques could be applied to unauthorized targets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Enumeration commands are demonstrated but skill explicitly states lab environment use case

low_findings.title: Self-Signed SSL Certificates
low_findings.description: Skill generates self-signed certificates for HTTPS testing. While appropriate for labs, this pattern should not be used in production.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Self-signed cert generation is explicitly documented in skill

low_findings.title: Service Enablement at Boot
low_findings.description: Skill configures services (apache2, smbd, snmpd) to start automatically via systemctl enable.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard system administration practice for lab servers

content.value_statement: This skill helps security learners set up vulnerable network services in an isolated lab environment for hands-on penetration testing practice. It provides step-by-step instructions for configuring HTTP, HTTPS, SNMP, and SMB services with known configurations for safe testing.
actual_capabilities: Configure Apache HTTP and HTTPS servers with self-signed certificates
Set up SNMP service with community strings for enumeration practice
Configure Samba SMB shares with various permission levels
Use nmap, snmpwalk, enum4linux, and smbclient for network enumeration
Analyze HTTP and SMB logs for security insights
Create isolated lab environments for security testing
limitations: Only for authorized, isolated lab environments - never use on production systems or unauthorized networks
Does not include actual exploitation techniques - only enumeration and setup
Self-signed certificates cause browser warnings and are not suitable for production
Default credentials configured in skill must never be used in production

use_cases.title: Security Certification Prep
use_cases.description: Security professionals preparing for CEH, OSCP, or CompTIA Security+ can use this skill to set up practice environments for enumeration techniques.

use_cases.title: Cybersecurity Training Labs
use_cases.description: Instructors can use this skill to quickly configure vulnerable target systems for hands-on training sessions.

use_cases.title: Home Lab Security Practice
use_cases.description: Learners can set up their own home labs to practice network reconnaissance and service enumeration safely.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Lab Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the network-101 skill to set up an HTTP server on port 80 for my penetration testing lab.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to configure a basic web server for testing

prompt_templates.title: SNMP Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure SNMP service with community strings for enumeration practice. Show me the commands to set up snmpd on Linux.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to set up SNMP for security testing

prompt_templates.title: SMB Share Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a Samba share with anonymous access for my lab environment. Include steps for configuring permissions.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to create SMB target for enumeration practice

prompt_templates.title: Enumeration Commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to enumerate the SMB service using enum4linux and smbmap. My target IP is 192.168.1.100.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to practice network enumeration techniques

output_examples.input: Set up HTTP server for testing
output_examples.output: Commands to install Apache, start the service, configure firewall rules for port 80, and verify the server is running using curl.
Steps to install snmpd, configure community strings, restart the service, and test with snmpwalk commands.
Example commands using smbclient, smbmap, and enum4linux to discover shares, users, and policies on a target system.

best_practices: Always use this skill in isolated, air-gapped lab environments separate from production networks
Document all changes made to lab systems and maintain separate credentials from production
Regularly rebuild lab systems to ensure clean, known states for testing
anti_patterns: Never apply configurations from this skill to production or internet-facing systems
Do not use default community strings (public/private) outside of isolated labs
Avoid leaving services enabled with chmod 777 permissions on any network

faq.question: Is this skill safe to use?
faq.answer: This skill is safe when used ONLY in isolated lab environments for authorized security testing. Never apply these configurations to production systems or networks you do not own.

faq.question: What tools does this skill cover?
faq.answer: The skill covers nmap for port scanning, snmpwalk for SNMP enumeration, smbclient and smbmap for SMB enumeration, and enum4linux for comprehensive SMB/NetBIOS enumeration.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for OSCP exam prep?
faq.answer: Yes, this skill is excellent for OSCP and other penetration testing certification preparation. It helps you understand how services are configured and enumerated.

faq.question: Does this skill perform exploitation?
faq.answer: No, this skill only covers service configuration and enumeration techniques, not exploitation. It sets up vulnerable targets for safe practice.

faq.question: What operating systems are supported?
faq.answer: The skill covers both Linux (Apache, Snmpd, Samba) and Windows (IIS, Windows SNMP Service, Windows SMB) configurations.

faq.question: Why are default credentials used in this skill?
faq.answer: Default SNMP communities (public/private) and anonymous SMB access are configured intentionally for lab practice. These represent common misconfigurations that security testers encounter.
